sections.plain_summary: Hydrogen sulphide SIBO (H2S SIBO) is a form of small intestinal bacterial overgrowth in which bacteria in the small intestine produce hydrogen sulphide gas rather than — or in addition to — the more commonly tested hydrogen or methane gases. It is associated with a distinct symptom pattern dominated by diarrhoea, urgency, and gas with a characteristic rotten-egg smell. Until recently it could not be directly detected by standard breath tests, which meant many people with this pattern were told their tests were 'flat' or normal and were left without a diagnosis. Newer testing options are beginning to close that gap.
sections.detailed_summary:
In a healthy gut, the small intestine contains relatively few bacteria. In SIBO, bacteria colonise the small intestine in excessive numbers, fermenting carbohydrates before they can be properly absorbed and producing gases that cause symptoms. Most SIBO research and clinical practice has focused on hydrogen-producing bacteria (associated with diarrhoea) and methane-producing archaea (associated with constipation). Hydrogen sulphide SIBO represents a third — and historically invisible — gas type.

Hydrogen sulphide is produced primarily by sulphate-reducing bacteria (SRBs) such as Desulfovibrio species, as well as by some Fusobacterium and Bilophila wadsworthia strains. These bacteria metabolise sulphur-containing compounds — from dietary sources such as meat, eggs, cruciferous vegetables, alliums, and sulphite food additives — and release H2S as a byproduct.

H2S is a biologically active gas. At low concentrations it acts as a signalling molecule involved in gut motility and mucosal protection, but at elevated levels it may impair colonocyte energy metabolism, increase intestinal permeability, affect motility, and exert toxic effects on the intestinal mucosa. The characteristic symptom pattern — loose, urgent, often foul-smelling stools and sulphurous gas — reflects both accelerated transit and local mucosal irritation.

For years, H2S SIBO was suspected but largely undetectable in clinical practice. The standard lactulose or glucose breath test measures only hydrogen and methane; H2S was not captured. People with this pattern often showed 'flat' breath tests — low hydrogen and methane — which was sometimes called the 'flat-line pattern' and informally linked to H2S production. A three-gas breath test that also measures hydrogen sulphide has become commercially available and is beginning to be used in specialist settings, though it is not yet widely standardised.

Because H2S SIBO sits at the intersection of microbial overgrowth, dietary sulphur metabolism, and gut motility, addressing it typically requires a multi-pronged approach: reducing sulphur substrate availability through diet, targeting sulphate-reducing bacteria with appropriate antimicrobials, and addressing any underlying motility or anatomical drivers that allowed overgrowth to develop in the first place.

prevalence.summary: Unknown precisely, but likely a significant subset of people with IBS-D and unexplained chronic diarrhoea — possibly more common than current diagnosis rates suggest.
prevalence.estimate: No validated population figure exists; some researchers estimate H2S-type patterns may account for 15–30% of SIBO presentations, but this is speculative and based on indirect evidence.
prevalence.notes: Because H2S was not measurable on standard tests until recently, prevalence data are extremely limited. The 'flat-line' breath test pattern — historically seen in roughly 10–15% of people undergoing SIBO breath testing — may partially represent H2S producers, but this is not confirmed. Research is ongoing. Women with IBS-D and people with a history of food poisoning or gastroenteritis appear to be over-represented in clinical descriptions, but formal epidemiological data are lacking.

subgroups.name: Pure H2S SIBO (flat-line pattern)
subgroups.description: Individuals whose breath tests show near-zero hydrogen and methane despite significant symptoms, historically described as the 'flat-line' pattern and now suspected to represent active H2S production consuming available hydrogen before it can be exhaled.
distinguishing_features: Low or undetectable hydrogen and methane on standard breath tests
May test positive on newer three-gas breath tests

subgroups.name: Mixed H2S and hydrogen SIBO
subgroups.description: Individuals who show elevated hydrogen on breath testing alongside an H2S component, with a symptom pattern that includes both diarrhoea and bloating.
distinguishing_features: Elevated hydrogen on standard breath test
Symptoms more complex than pure diarrhoea — may include significant bloating
Requires treatment approaches addressing both gas types

subgroups.name: Post-infectious H2S SIBO
subgroups.description: A subgroup in whom H2S-producing overgrowth developed following a bout of acute gastroenteritis or food poisoning, consistent with post-infectious IBS mechanisms.
distinguishing_features: Clear symptom onset following an identifiable gut infection
May have anti-CdtB or anti-vinculin antibodies on blood testing
Often has associated impaired migrating motor complex (MMC) function

subgroups.name: H2S SIBO with high dietary sulphur intake
subgroups.description: Individuals in whom symptom severity is strongly modulated by dietary sulphur, including high meat intake, eggs, cruciferous vegetables, alliums, or processed foods with sulphite additives.
distinguishing_features: Strong correlation between high-sulphur meals and symptom flares
Symptoms may partially improve with low-sulphur diet even without antimicrobial treatment
Egg-white odour in gas is particularly prominent

symptoms.notes: Often urgent and difficult to defer; may alternate with brief periods of normal stool but rarely tends toward constipation as the dominant pattern.
Many people describe needing to locate a bathroom immediately after eating or in the morning; this can severely impact daily functioning and cause significant anxiety.
One of the most characteristic features; the odour reflects actual H2S gas production and is often described as distinctly different from typical flatulence.
May be less pronounced than in hydrogen or methane SIBO but is commonly reported; often worse after sulphur-containing foods.
Often lower abdominal; may be relieved temporarily by passing gas or having a bowel movement.
Can occur particularly after eating; not universal but reported by a meaningful proportion of people with H2S-type SIBO.
H2S at elevated levels may interfere with mitochondrial function; fatigue in SIBO generally may also relate to nutrient malabsorption and systemic inflammation.
Reported by many people with SIBO of all types; the mechanism is not fully established but may involve systemic absorption of bacterial metabolites or secondary nutritional deficiencies.

symptoms.symptom: Food sensitivities and worsening after specific foods
symptoms.notes: Sulphur-containing foods (eggs, meat, cruciferous vegetables, onions, garlic, wine, dried fruit) tend to be particularly problematic; this pattern can help distinguish H2S SIBO from other types.
The sensation that a bowel movement is not fully complete; may contribute to ongoing urgency.
Sometimes present; if blood is also visible, other diagnoses including IBD must be ruled out.

symptoms.symptom: Weight loss or difficulty maintaining weight
symptoms.notes: Reflects malabsorption of fats, carbohydrates, and potentially proteins when overgrowth is significant.
Gut-brain axis effects are well-documented in SIBO generally; H2S specifically may have neurological signalling roles that are disrupted when levels are dysregulated.

key_patterns: Flat-line breath test (near-zero hydrogen and methane throughout a standard lactulose or glucose breath test) with ongoing significant symptoms — historically the most common indirect signal for H2S SIBO
Positive three-gas breath test showing elevated hydrogen sulphide at one or more time points following lactulose or glucose challenge
Strong correlation of symptom flares with sulphur-rich foods (eggs, red meat, cruciferous vegetables, onions, garlic, wine, dried fruit, processed meats with sulphites)
IBS-D or chronic diarrhoea that has not responded to standard hydrogen-SIBO treatment (rifaximin alone) or methane-SIBO treatment
Post-infectious IBS pattern with diarrhoea onset following acute gastroenteritis

at_home.name: Low-sulphur dietary trial
at_home.description: Systematically reducing dietary sulphur for 1–2 weeks by avoiding eggs, red meat, cruciferous vegetables (broccoli, cauliflower, cabbage, kale), alliums (onion, garlic, leek), wine, beer, dried fruit, and processed meats. Keeping a symptom diary to track gas odour, urgency, and stool consistency during this period. Partial or significant improvement during the trial provides informal supporting evidence for H2S involvement.
at_home.safety_notes: A low-sulphur diet is nutritionally restrictive if maintained long-term. This is intended as a short diagnostic trial, not a permanent dietary approach. People with existing dietary restrictions or eating disorder history should exercise caution.

at_home.name: Symptom and food diary
at_home.description: Tracking meals (with attention to sulphur-containing foods), stool frequency and consistency (using the Bristol Stool Scale), gas odour, urgency severity, and any systemic symptoms over 2–4 weeks. This record is valuable for identifying patterns and for presenting to practitioners.
at_home.safety_notes: No safety concerns; however, obsessive food tracking can increase health anxiety in some individuals — maintain a practical rather than exhaustive approach.

clinical.name: Three-gas lactulose or glucose breath test (H2/CH4/H2S)
clinical.description: A breath test that measures all three fermentation gases — hydrogen, methane, and hydrogen sulphide — at baseline and at regular intervals (typically every 20 minutes for 2–3 hours) following ingestion of a fermentable substrate. Elevated H2S readings above established thresholds at early time points suggest small intestinal rather than colonic production. The FoodMarble AIRE 2 device and the Trio-Smart breath test (developed by researchers including Dr Mark Pimentel) are among the available options.
clinical.availability: The Trio-Smart breath test is available direct-to-consumer in the United States and through some functional medicine and gastroenterology practitioners internationally. Not yet widely available through standard NHS or public health systems. Interpretation norms are still being established — results should be interpreted by a practitioner familiar with H2S breath testing.

clinical.name: Standard lactulose or glucose breath test (H2/CH4 only)
clinical.description: The conventional two-gas breath test used to diagnose hydrogen and methane SIBO. A 'flat-line' result (hydrogen consistently below 10–12 ppm, methane below 3 ppm throughout) in a symptomatic individual may warrant suspicion of H2S SIBO, since sulphate-reducing bacteria can consume available hydrogen and prevent its accumulation in exhaled breath.
clinical.availability: Available through gastroenterologists, some GPs, and directly through private labs or functional medicine practitioners. Widely available in the UK, US, and Australia.

clinical.name: Comprehensive stool analysis with microbiome profiling
clinical.description: Advanced stool testing (e.g. from Genova Diagnostics, Doctor's Data, Diagnostic Solutions GI-MAP, or Biomesight) can identify relative abundance of sulphate-reducing bacterial species including Desulfovibrio and Bilophila wadsworthia. While this reflects colonic rather than small intestinal microbiome, elevated SRB populations alongside compatible symptoms may support the clinical picture.
clinical.availability: Available privately through functional medicine practitioners and some integrative gastroenterologists. Not routinely ordered in mainstream NHS or standard gastroenterology practice. Interpretation requires familiarity with functional stool testing.

clinical.name: Anti-CdtB and anti-vinculin antibody blood test
clinical.description: A blood test measuring autoantibodies linked to post-infectious IBS and impaired migrating motor complex function — a known driver of SIBO recurrence. Positive results do not diagnose H2S SIBO specifically but can identify post-infectious mechanisms and support a more aggressive treatment and motility-focused approach.
clinical.availability: The ibs-smart test (by Gemelli Biotech) is available in the United States; availability varies internationally. Some functional medicine practitioners can order it.

clinical.name: Colonoscopy and small bowel imaging
clinical.description: Used to rule out structural causes of diarrhoea including IBD, microscopic colitis, or anatomical abnormalities. Not diagnostic for H2S SIBO itself, but important for excluding other serious pathology — particularly when there is blood in stool, unintended weight loss, or symptoms beginning after age 50.
clinical.availability: Available through gastroenterologists in standard care settings.

related_conditions.name: SIBO (Small intestinal bacterial overgrowth)
related_conditions.explanation: H2S SIBO is a subtype of SIBO defined by the specific gas produced. The underlying mechanism — bacterial overgrowth in the small intestine — is the same, but the microbial species and metabolic pathways involved differ from hydrogen or methane SIBO.

related_conditions.name: Hydrogen SIBO
related_conditions.explanation: Both produce diarrhoea-predominant symptoms and involve fermentation in the small intestine. They can co-exist. H2S SIBO may be misclassified as hydrogen SIBO or missed entirely when using standard two-gas testing.

related_conditions.name: Methane SIBO
related_conditions.explanation: Methane SIBO (constipation-dominant) can co-exist with H2S-producing bacteria in some individuals, producing a mixed picture. Methanogenic archaea and sulphate-reducing bacteria compete for available hydrogen in different ways.

related_conditions.name: IBS (Irritable bowel syndrome)
related_conditions.explanation: Most people with H2S SIBO will have been given an IBS-D diagnosis at some point. H2S SIBO may be a root cause underlying a significant proportion of IBS-D cases, and the two labels frequently coexist because IBS is a symptom-based diagnosis rather than a mechanistic one.

related_conditions.name: Gut dysbiosis
related_conditions.explanation: H2S SIBO reflects a specific form of dysbiosis — overgrowth of sulphate-reducing bacteria in the small intestine. Broader colonic dysbiosis with elevated SRB populations frequently accompanies or precedes small intestinal involvement.

related_conditions.name: Gastroparesis
related_conditions.explanation: Delayed gastric emptying allows bacteria to remain in the upper GI tract for longer, creating conditions that favour overgrowth. Gastroparesis is one of several motility disorders that can predispose to all forms of SIBO including H2S SIBO.

related_conditions.name: Exocrine pancreatic insufficiency
related_conditions.explanation: EPI can produce loose, fatty, foul-smelling stools and diarrhoea that superficially resembles H2S SIBO. Both conditions can also coexist. Distinguishing them matters because treatment differs fundamentally.

related_conditions.name: Bile acid malabsorption
related_conditions.explanation: BAM causes urgent watery diarrhoea that closely resembles H2S SIBO symptoms. Both can follow infectious gastroenteritis or bowel surgery. Both are frequently missed in standard workups. Distinguishing them requires specific testing.

related_conditions.name: IBD in remission (Crohn's disease / ulcerative colitis)
related_conditions.explanation: Ongoing symptoms in IBD patients in apparent remission may partly reflect co-existing H2S SIBO or dysbiosis with elevated SRB populations rather than ongoing mucosal inflammation. Bilophila wadsworthia, an H2S producer, has been linked to gut inflammation in Crohn's disease research.

related_conditions.name: Histamine intolerance
related_conditions.explanation: Increased intestinal permeability associated with SIBO can impair histamine clearance, and dysbiotic bacteria can produce histamine directly. Histamine intolerance commonly co-exists with SIBO, adding a layer of food reactivity on top of the sulphur-specific pattern.

related_conditions.name: Mast cell activation syndrome
related_conditions.explanation: H2S and other bacterial metabolites may activate mucosal mast cells, and MCAS is increasingly recognised as a comorbidity in people with complex, treatment-resistant SIBO. Symptom overlap is substantial.

related_conditions.name: POTS / dysautonomia
related_conditions.explanation: Autonomic dysfunction can impair gut motility, including the migrating motor complex that normally clears the small intestine between meals. POTS and other dysautonomic conditions are over-represented among people with recurrent SIBO.

related_conditions.name: Hypermobile EDS / HSD
related_conditions.explanation: Connective tissue laxity in hEDS can affect the gut wall and autonomic function, impairing normal intestinal motility and creating vulnerability to recurrent SIBO of all types.

related_conditions.name: Endometriosis
related_conditions.explanation: Endometriosis involving the bowel can alter gut motility and microbiome composition. SIBO, including H2S-type patterns, is reported at elevated rates in people with endometriosis, and symptoms frequently overlap.

related_conditions.name: Central sensitisation / visceral hypersensitivity
related_conditions.explanation: Long-standing gut inflammation and motility disruption from SIBO can sensitise the enteric and central nervous systems, meaning that even after bacterial overgrowth is treated, visceral hypersensitivity may persist and require its own management.

related_conditions.name: Anxiety disorder
related_conditions.explanation: The gut-brain axis bidirectionally connects gut dysfunction and anxiety. H2S and other bacterial metabolites may influence neurotransmitter function; conversely, chronic stress and anxiety can impair gut motility and perpetuate SIBO.

related_conditions.name: Coeliac disease
related_conditions.explanation: Untreated or undertreated coeliac disease damages the small intestinal mucosa and impairs motility, creating conditions that favour bacterial overgrowth. H2S SIBO may develop secondary to coeliac disease.

strategies.name: Low-sulphur diet
strategies.description: Reducing dietary sulphur substrate available to sulphate-reducing bacteria can meaningfully reduce gas production and symptom severity. Foods to reduce or temporarily eliminate include eggs, red meat, cruciferous vegetables (broccoli, cauliflower, cabbage, Brussels sprouts, kale), alliums (onion, garlic, leek, shallots), wine and beer (particularly sulphite-containing wine), dried fruits, and processed meats and foods containing added sulphites (E220–E228). This approach targets the fuel source for H2S-producing bacteria rather than the bacteria themselves.
strategies.notes: A strict low-sulphur diet is not nutritionally sustainable long-term and excludes many otherwise healthy foods. It is most useful as a short-term symptom management tool during treatment or as a maintenance measure at a less restrictive level. A FODMAP-trained registered dietitian can help design a workable version.

strategies.name: Targeted antimicrobial treatment (rifaximin ± bismuth)
strategies.description: Rifaximin is the most studied antibiotic for SIBO and has some activity against sulphate-reducing bacteria. Bismuth subsalicylate (the active ingredient in Pepto-Bismol) is of particular interest in H2S SIBO because bismuth binds hydrogen sulphide directly in the gut lumen, reducing H2S levels. Some practitioners use bismuth as an adjunct to rifaximin in H2S SIBO, or as a standalone symptomatic agent. Dosing and duration vary; this requires medical supervision.
strategies.notes: Bismuth compounds should not be used long-term without medical oversight. There are limited head-to-head trial data specifically for H2S SIBO treatment. Practitioners familiar with this subtype are more likely to consider bismuth than those focused only on standard SIBO protocols.

strategies.name: Herbal antimicrobial protocols
strategies.description: Certain herbal antimicrobials have demonstrated activity against sulphate-reducing bacteria and are used in naturopathic and functional medicine practice as alternatives or adjuncts to pharmaceutical antibiotics. Allicin (from garlic extract — notably in an odourless form), berberine, and oregano oil are most commonly discussed. These are typically used in combination and cycled over several weeks.
strategies.notes: Evidence for herbal protocols in H2S SIBO specifically is largely anecdotal; some general SIBO studies suggest comparable efficacy to rifaximin for overall SIBO, but H2S-specific data are sparse. Individual responses vary considerably. Herbal antimicrobials can still cause side effects and interactions — oversight from a knowledgeable practitioner is advisable.

strategies.name: Prokinetics to support migrating motor complex (MMC) function
strategies.description: The migrating motor complex — a wave of muscular activity that sweeps the small intestine clean between meals — is impaired in many people with recurrent SIBO. Addressing this is critical to preventing re-overgrowth after treatment. Options include low-dose naltrexone (LDN), prucalopride, erythromycin at low dose, iberogast (herbal), and ginger root. These are used after or alongside antimicrobial treatment.
strategies.notes: Prokinetics are one of the most important factors in long-term SIBO management and are often under-emphasised in conventional care. A practitioner willing to assess and treat motility dysfunction — not just the overgrowth itself — makes a significant difference to outcomes.

strategies.name: Spacing meals and avoiding snacking (time-restricted eating)
strategies.description: The MMC operates between meals, typically requiring at least 4–5 hours of fasting to complete a full sweep of the small intestine. Frequent snacking disrupts this process and allows bacteria to accumulate. Eating 3 distinct meals with no snacking in between, and allowing a longer overnight fast, can support natural intestinal clearance.
strategies.notes: This is one of the simplest and most mechanistically sound lifestyle adjustments for SIBO prevention. It conflicts with the common advice to 'eat little and often' for digestive comfort — for SIBO patients, that advice may worsen the underlying condition.

strategies.name: Elemental diet (short-term)
strategies.description: An elemental or semi-elemental formula diet provides nutrition in a pre-digested form that is absorbed very high in the small intestine, leaving little substrate for bacterial fermentation. A 2–3 week elemental diet course has been shown in research to significantly reduce SIBO bacterial load. It is demanding to complete but does not require a prescription.
strategies.notes: The elemental diet is often used when antibiotic courses have failed or cannot be tolerated. It is nutritionally monotonous and adherence is challenging. Medical supervision is recommended, particularly for people with low body weight, diabetes, or other metabolic considerations.

strategies.name: Bismuth subsalicylate as symptomatic relief
strategies.description: Bismuth subsalicylate (Pepto-Bismol) directly binds H2S in the gut lumen and can reduce gas odour and symptom severity in the short term. It is available over the counter and can be useful as a symptomatic measure while awaiting or between treatments.
strategies.notes: Not suitable for long-term use. Contains salicylate — avoid in those with aspirin sensitivity or who take blood thinners. Not appropriate in children or pregnant individuals.

strategies.name: Stress management and nervous system regulation
strategies.description: The enteric nervous system is directly modulated by stress and autonomic tone. Chronic stress impairs MMC function and gut motility. Practices including diaphragmatic breathing, vagal nerve stimulation approaches, mindfulness-based stress reduction, and regular gentle movement can support gut motility and reduce symptom severity.
strategies.notes: This is not a substitute for addressing microbial overgrowth directly, but the nervous system component is often undervalued. People with a history of trauma or chronic anxiety may benefit from working with a therapist alongside gut-focused treatment.

things_to_avoid: Eating frequently throughout the day or snacking constantly — this prevents the migrating motor complex from completing its cleansing sweeps and maintains conditions that favour bacterial overgrowth

High-sulphur foods in excess during active treatment — eggs, red meat, cruciferous vegetables, onions, garlic, wine, dried fruit, and sulphite-preserved foods provide the substrate that H2S-producing bacteria ferment
Proton pump inhibitors (PPIs) unless medically necessary — acid suppression reduces a key defence against upper GI bacterial colonisation and is associated with increased SIBO risk

Probiotic supplements without guidance — while some probiotics may help in SIBO, others (particularly Lactobacillus-heavy preparations) may worsen D-lactic acidosis or introduce more fermentable activity in the small intestine; Saccharomyces boulardii is generally better tolerated as a starting point

Long-term very low-FODMAP diet without reassessment — while helpful for symptom control, a highly restrictive FODMAP diet can reduce microbiome diversity and deprive beneficial bacteria of fermentable fuel; it is a management tool, not a cure

Assuming a negative standard two-gas breath test rules out SIBO — a flat-line result in the presence of characteristic symptoms should raise suspicion of H2S SIBO, not be taken as reassurance that the gut is normal

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Most gastroenterologists are not testing for H2S SIBO and are not familiar enough with it to manage it well. A standard workup will miss it, and a standard IBS diagnosis does not exclude it.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: People want to believe that a gastroenterology referral and a colonoscopy means they have been thoroughly investigated. Accepting that standard care has significant blind spots requires seeking further help from outside the mainstream, which takes time, money, and self-advocacy.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: People who identify a practitioner familiar with three-gas breath testing and SIBO subtypes — often a functional medicine doctor or an integrative gastroenterologist — are able to get a more complete picture of what is driving their symptoms and access targeted treatment protocols rather than indefinite symptom management.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Treating the bacterial overgrowth alone is rarely enough. Without addressing the underlying motility dysfunction or other driver that allowed overgrowth to develop, H2S SIBO typically recurs within weeks to months of completing antimicrobial treatment.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: After completing a demanding antibiotic or herbal protocol and experiencing improvement, it feels like the problem has been solved. Accepting that ongoing management — prokinetics, meal spacing, dietary structure — is necessary feels discouraging.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: People who accept the recurrence-prevention framework and build prokinetic support and meal-spacing habits into their long-term routine report significantly longer remission periods and are less devastated when minor symptom returns occur, because they understand the mechanism and know how to respond.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: There is no single cure. H2S SIBO management involves sustained behavioural, dietary, and often pharmacological interventions — not a one-time fix.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: After years of suffering, the hope of a definitive treatment (one course of antibiotics, one diet) is emotionally compelling. The idea of a chronic management approach feels like accepting defeat.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: People who reframe the condition as manageable rather than curable — and who build a sustainable toolkit of dietary habits, prokinetics, and monitoring — typically achieve stable, functional remission with much less disruption to daily life than those still searching for a cure.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: The connection between diet and symptoms in H2S SIBO is more specific than general 'gut health' advice captures. Generic low-FODMAP diets, clean eating, or elimination of gluten may provide partial relief but will not address the sulphur substrate issue that drives H2S production.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Dietary interventions that help with bloating (like low-FODMAP) feel like they should be sufficient. Identifying that specific sulphur-containing foods — including some that are widely considered healthy, like eggs and broccoli — are driving symptoms requires a more nuanced understanding than most dietary advice provides.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: People who specifically map their sulphur intake against symptom severity, and who work with a dietitian to design a nutritionally complete lower-sulphur eating pattern, often achieve symptom control that eluded them on more generic gut health diets.

key_levers.lever: Accurate diagnosis with three-gas breath testing
key_levers.why_it_matters: Without confirming H2S involvement, treatment is guesswork. Many people have been through multiple rounds of hydrogen-SIBO or methane-SIBO treatment that simply did not address the actual gas type driving their symptoms.
key_levers.how_to_start: Research availability of the Trio-Smart breath test or equivalent three-gas test through a functional medicine practitioner or directly. If unavailable, document the flat-line pattern on standard testing alongside symptoms and present this to a knowledgeable practitioner.
key_levers.timeline: Testing can typically be completed within days to weeks once access is arranged; results are immediate.

key_levers.lever: Targeted antimicrobial treatment with bismuth as an adjunct
key_levers.why_it_matters: Addressing the bacterial overgrowth directly — with a protocol that accounts for H2S-producing species rather than assuming hydrogen-type SIBO — produces more meaningful symptom resolution than symptom-only management.
key_levers.how_to_start: Work with a practitioner (functional medicine doctor, integrative gastroenterologist) familiar with H2S SIBO to discuss rifaximin with or without bismuth subsalicylate, or herbal antimicrobial alternatives.
key_levers.timeline: Most antimicrobial protocols last 4–6 weeks; symptom improvement during treatment can begin within the first week to two weeks.

key_levers.why_it_matters: The migrating motor complex is the gut's self-cleaning mechanism. Without restoring its function — through pharmaceutical prokinetics, herbal options, or meal spacing — bacterial overgrowth is highly likely to return, often within 1–3 months.
key_levers.how_to_start: Discuss prokinetic options with a practitioner. Begin meal spacing (4–5 hours between meals, no snacking) immediately — this is free, evidence-supported, and can begin today.
key_levers.timeline: Meal spacing effects on motility may be felt within 1–2 weeks. Pharmaceutical prokinetics typically require 4–8 weeks of consistent use to assess effect.

key_levers.why_it_matters: Reducing the substrate available to H2S-producing bacteria limits gas production at source. Even partial reduction in dietary sulphur can significantly reduce symptom severity, particularly gas odour and urgency.
key_levers.how_to_start: For two weeks, systematically reduce the highest-sulphur foods (eggs, red meat, cruciferous vegetables, onions, garlic, wine, dried fruit, processed meats) and keep a symptom diary tracking gas odour, urgency, and stool consistency. Review the results.
key_levers.timeline: Dietary changes can produce noticeable symptom shifts within 3–7 days.

key_levers.lever: Identifying and addressing the underlying driver (root cause)
key_levers.why_it_matters: H2S SIBO does not arise without a reason. Common drivers include impaired MMC function (often post-infectious), connective tissue disorders (hEDS), hypothyroidism, adhesions from surgery or endometriosis, and anatomical variants. Treating overgrowth without identifying the driver leads to recurrence.
key_levers.how_to_start: Review personal history for post-infectious onset, relevant surgeries, connective tissue symptoms, thyroid symptoms, or hormonal factors. Bring this history to a practitioner for systematic investigation.
key_levers.timeline: Root-cause investigation may take weeks to months depending on what is found; addressing identified drivers is typically a longer-term process.

common_mistakes: Treating only with rifaximin (designed for hydrogen SIBO) and not adjusting protocol for H2S-specific bacteria — this frequently produces incomplete or short-lived improvement
Stopping treatment as soon as symptoms resolve and not building in prokinetic support — this is the most common reason for rapid relapse

Following a strict low-FODMAP diet as the primary treatment strategy without addressing bacterial overgrowth — FODMAP restriction manages symptoms by reducing fermentable substrate but does not eradicate the underlying overgrowth
Assuming that because eggs and cruciferous vegetables are 'healthy', they cannot be problematic — in H2S SIBO specifically, sulphur-rich whole foods can be among the most significant triggers
Taking Lactobacillus-heavy probiotic supplements without guidance — these can worsen symptoms in some SIBO presentations and are not automatically beneficial
Pursuing repeated antibiotic courses without ever investigating or treating the motility dysfunction driving recurrence — this leads to diminishing returns and risk of antimicrobial resistance
Accepting an IBS-D diagnosis as a final answer rather than a starting point for further investigation

specialists.type: Functional medicine doctor or integrative physician
specialists.role: Most likely to be familiar with H2S SIBO as a distinct subtype, to have access to three-gas breath testing, and to use a root-cause investigative framework that includes motility assessment, post-infectious testing, and bismuth-based protocols.
specialists.notes: Quality varies significantly. Look for practitioners who specifically mention SIBO subtypes and breath testing in their practice descriptions. The Institute for Functional Medicine (IFM) maintains a practitioner directory.

specialists.role: Can rule out IBD, microscopic colitis, and structural disease; may be familiar with newer breath testing and SIBO treatment protocols. Most valuable when they go beyond symptom management to investigate mechanisms.
specialists.notes: Many mainstream gastroenterologists are not yet familiar with H2S SIBO specifically and may not offer three-gas breath testing. Asking directly about H2S breath testing or three-gas testing before booking can save time.

specialists.role: Indispensable for navigating the dietary complexity of H2S SIBO — designing a low-sulphur approach that is also nutritionally adequate, managing any co-existing FODMAP sensitivities, and avoiding the common trap of an overly restrictive diet that becomes unsustainable.
specialists.notes: Look for dietitians with specific experience in SIBO, not just IBS. Monash University and King's College London both certify FODMAP-trained dietitians; some have additional SIBO training.

specialists.type: Naturopathic doctor (ND) with gut health specialisation
specialists.role: Can offer herbal antimicrobial protocols, prokinetic support, and comprehensive dietary guidance for those preferring or unable to access pharmaceutical options. Most familiar with the bismuth and herbal protocol approach to H2S SIBO.
specialists.notes: Regulation and training standards for NDs vary significantly by country. Most useful when working alongside, rather than instead of, medical investigation to rule out serious pathology.

specialists.type: Autonomic neurologist or cardiologist (for POTS/dysautonomia assessment)
specialists.role: Relevant for people with co-existing POTS or autonomic dysfunction, where impaired gut motility driving SIBO recurrence may have a neurological basis requiring its own treatment.
specialists.notes: Relevant for a subset of people with H2S SIBO — those with co-existing lightheadedness, heart rate abnormalities on standing, or known connective tissue disorders.

patient_communities.name: r/SIBO
patient_communities.notes: The largest active SIBO patient community online. H2S SIBO is regularly discussed; there are pinned resources, treatment experience threads, and active discussion of three-gas testing. Quality of information varies — cross-reference with practitioner guidance.

patient_communities.name: SIBO SOS Community
patient_communities.notes: Community and educational resource founded by Shivan Sarna, with access to practitioner interviews, summit recordings, and patient stories. Covers H2S SIBO including interviews with researchers such as Dr Mark Pimentel.

patient_communities.name: The SIBO Doctor — patient resources
patient_communities.notes: Run by Dr Nirala Jacobi (ND), a naturopathic physician specialising in SIBO. Contains detailed educational content on H2S SIBO, treatment protocols, and practitioner-sourced guidance. Well-regarded in the SIBO patient and practitioner community.

patient_communities.name: IBS/SIBO Support Group
patient_communities.notes: Multiple active Facebook groups exist under similar names. Search for SIBO-specific groups rather than generic IBS groups for more targeted H2S SIBO discussion. Community experience with different practitioners and protocols can be valuable for navigating care.

learning_resources.title: Trio-Smart Breath Test — educational content
learning_resources.description: The developers of the three-gas breath test provide accessible information about hydrogen sulphide SIBO, the science behind H2S measurement, and how to interpret results. Directly relevant to anyone considering this test.

learning_resources.title: Fast Tract Digestion: IBS
learning_resources.description: A science-based dietary framework for SIBO that takes a fermentation potential approach. Provides a practical model for understanding how different foods drive gas production, including sulphur-related fermentation patterns.

learning_resources.title: The Gut Health Protocol
learning_resources.description: A comprehensive, patient-written guide to gut dysbiosis, SIBO, and H2S-producing bacteria. Written from a patient-researcher perspective with extensive literature references. Detailed and practical for people doing deep self-directed research.

learning_resources.title: SIBO SOS Summit recordings — H2S SIBO sessions
learning_resources.description: Interview-format presentations with leading SIBO researchers and clinicians, including sessions specifically on hydrogen sulphide SIBO with Dr Mark Pimentel and Dr Nirala Jacobi. Accessible to non-specialists and useful for understanding the current state of research.

learning_resources.title: Hydrogen Sulfide Overproduction and Its Potential Role in IBS — research overview
learning_resources.description: PubMed search results for H2S in SIBO and IBS contexts. Key papers include work by Pimentel and colleagues on the three-gas breath test and on H2S's role in gut function. Reading abstracts provides a solid grounding in the current evidence base.

learning_resources.title: The SIBO Doctor Podcast
learning_resources.description: Practitioner-led podcast covering SIBO subtypes including H2S SIBO, treatment protocols, dietary approaches, and patient case discussions. One of the most consistently useful audio resources for people with SIBO wanting to understand their condition at a deeper level.

learning_resources.title: Dropping Acid: The Reflux Diet Cookbook and Cure — SIBO connection chapter
learning_resources.author_or_source: Jamie Kaufman MD, Jordan Stern MD
learning_resources.description: While focused on reflux, this resource is relevant because acid suppression (PPIs) is a major SIBO risk factor and the relationship between H2S SIBO and upper GI symptoms is underappreciated.

sections.relevance_signal: Chronic digestive symptoms — particularly diarrhoea, urgent loose stools, foul-smelling gas, and bloating — that have not responded to standard IBS treatment or conventional hydrogen/methane SIBO protocols, and may have been dismissed or misdiagnosed for years.
